paper_id: Goncalves et al. - 2011 - Using an ECG reference ontology for semantic interoperability of ECG data
input_limitations: The markdown extraction contains encoding/OCR artifacts and some merged words, but the main sections and evidence are readable.

evidence_quotes.quote: In this paper we test the hypothesis that a domain reference ontology of the electrocardiogram (ECG) can be employed in an effective manner to achieve semantic integration between ECG data standards.

We have conducted an integration experiment which provides evidence that the ECG Ontology can be effectively used to support the design of interoperable versions of the ECG standards AHA/MIT-BIH, SCP-ECG and HL7 aECG.

goals.justification: The ontology is explicitly used as an instrument for semantic integration and to support design of interoperable versions of ECG standards, matching the Intervening signal of enabling interoperability.

evidence_quotes.quote: For reference ontology [10], we mean an ontology resulting from an application-independent representation of a given domain.
In this case, the representation of what the ECG is , on both sides of the patient and of the physician.

goals.justification: The ontology is framed as a domain representation of what ECG is, supporting domain conceptualization and shared understanding.

evidence_quotes.quote: From the results presented in Table 4, one could (i) propose conceptual and terminological changes in the corresponding standards in order to facilitate the unification of these standards; or even (ii) have a starting point for an EHR unification program that would be grounded in reality since its very beginning (as proposed by Smith [7, p. 297]), while still being sensitive to the existing conceptualizations and terminologies proposed by standardization initiatives.

goals.justification: Beyond implementation-oriented interoperability, the ontology-based results are used to support redesign and unification decisions for standards; the evidence is phrased as an application rather than a completed decision process.

evidence_quotes.quote: As a reference model whose primary goal is adherence to reality, an ontology can serve as an anchor to both conceptual and terminological negotiation processes.

uncertain_goals.reason: The phrase suggests negotiation between parties, but the paper does not clearly describe synchronous communication or an actual stakeholder communication setting.

models.model_label: Elicited conceptual models of ECG standards

evidence_quotes.quote: Our hypothesis is tested by means of an integration experiment that uses, on the one hand, an ECG Ontology and, on the other hand, elicited conceptual models of the ECG standards.
Once we have excavated the conceptual models underlying the ECG data formats, we are able to perform their semantic integration by using the ECG Ontology.

goals.justification: The elicited conceptual models are directly used in an integration experiment to achieve semantic integration of ECG data formats, which fits Intervening.

evidence_quotes.quote: We use here the term ''Conceptual Model Excavation' to refer to the activity of, firstly, making explicit these implicit conceptualizations, followed by their representations via concrete engineering artifacts, i.e., conceptual models.
The ECG conceptualizations underlying the standards are extracted from their specifications based on the textual descriptions of their composing elements and relations.

goals.justification: The models are created to make implicit conceptualizations explicit, which is a direct domain understanding and clarification goal.

evidence_quotes.quote: Eventhough the excavated conceptual models we convey here are not meant to be final representations of those standards, to have them expressed in an explicit manner puts forth concrete media for discussion and elaboration in the community of Biomedical Informatics.

goals.justification: The authors explicitly state that the models provide media for discussion and elaboration in a community, suggesting communication among distinct stakeholders.
